reasoning: All four correctly hit the core flaws (post hoc/confounding, undecomposed revenue-per-employee ratio, survivorship-biased competitor cherry-pick, false-dichotomy remedy, ignored attrition costs), so they cluster high on basic rigor and usefulness; the separation comes from calibration and bias awareness. C wins decisively: it is the only response that quantifies confidence twice (primary-cause probability "well under 30%," best-fix probability "lower still"), names the widest set of distinct biases including resulting and regression to the mean, and adds genuine epistemic discipline with a pre-registered falsifier and a premortem on the mandate. A is a very close second with the sharpest second-order insight (a 5-day mandate could drive revenue/employee further down via top-performer attrition, the cure worsening the cited metric) and explicit naming of motivated reasoning, but it offers only a qualitative "Weak" verdict. D is strong and crisp, with the cleanest counterfactual test, the full 2x2, an irreversibility point, and real output-metric alternatives, but it surfaces fewer distinct biases and only a vague "low" confidence. B is thorough and well-structured with the best cost-benefit pilot framing, yet it is the most conventional: no quantified confidence, no falsifier, and fewer named biases, making it the least differentiated of an excellent field.
